{{Short description|Concept in psychology}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=May 2024}}
According to the model of the '''five stages of grief''', or the '''Kübler-Ross model''', those experiencing sudden [[grief]] following an abrupt realization (shock) go through five emotions: [[denial]], [[anger]], [[bargaining]], [[depression (mood)|depression]], and [[acceptance]]. Though widely used, the theory is empirically unsupported.<ref name="Stroebe2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Stroebe M, Schut H, Boerner K | title = Cautioning Health-Care Professionals | journal = Omega | volume = 74 | issue = 4 | pages = 455–473 | date = March 2017 | pmid = 28355991 | pmc = 5375020 | doi = 10.1177/0030222817691870 }}</ref> 

Introduced as "The Five Stages of Death" by Swiss-American [[psychiatrist]] [[Elisabeth Kübler-Ross]] in 1969,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kübler-Ross |first=Elisabeth |title=On Death & Dying |date=12 August 2014 |publisher=Simon and Schuster |isbn=978-1-4767-7554-8 |url=https://www.simonandschuster.com/books/On-Death-and-Dying/Elisabeth-Kubler-Ross/9781476775548}}</ref> this model has been known by various names, including "The Five Stages of Loss",<ref>{{Cite web |title=THE FIVE STAGES OF LOSS Trademark of Elisabeth Kubler-Ross Limited Family Partnership - Registration Number 6147870 - Serial Number 87685508 :: Justia Trademarks |url=http://trademarks.justia.com/876/85/the-five-stages-of-87685508.html |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=trademarks.justia.com |language=en}}</ref> "The Kübler-Ross Model",<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kubler-Ross Five Stage Model |url=https://www.change-management-coach.com/kubler-ross.html |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=Change Management Coach}}</ref> the "Kübler-Ross Grief Cycle",<ref>{{Cite web |title=5 Stages Of Grief Explained |url=https://mungfali.com/explore/5-Stages-of-Grief-Explained |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=Mungfali |language=en}}</ref> the "Grief Cycle",<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-06-04 |title=Guest Blog: Mental Health and Complex Grief in Survivorship |url=https://www.childrenscancercause.org/blog/mental-health-and-complex-grief-in-survivorship |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=Children's Cancer Cause |language=en-US}}</ref> "The Seven Stages of Grief",<ref>{{Cite web |last=Liuba |date=2023-09-19 |title=7 Stages of Grief: A Comprehensive Guide to Understanding Loss |url=https://psychology.tips/7-stages-of-grief/ |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=A Simplified Psychology Guide |language=en-US}}</ref> and the "Kübler-Ross Change Curve".<ref>{{Cite web |last=Tahir |first=Umar |date=2019-12-10 |title=What is Kubler Ross Change Curve? {{!}} Stages & Advantages |url=https://changemanagementinsight.com/kubler-ross-change-curve-model/ |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=CMI |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2015-06-24 |title=Understanding the Kubler-Ross Change Curve |url=https://cleverism.com/understanding-kubler-ross-change-curve/ |access-date=2024-07-27 |website=Cleverism |language=en}}</ref>

==History==
The model was introduced by Kübler-Ross in her 1969 book ''On Death and Dying'',<ref name=":0">{{cite book | vauthors = Kübler-Ross E | date = 1969 | title = On Death and Dying | publisher = Routledge | isbn = 0-415-04015-9 }}</ref> and was inspired by her work with [[Terminal illness|terminally ill]] patients.<ref>{{cite magazine |url=http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,689491,00.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090224074917/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,689491,00.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=February 24, 2009 |title= Milestones |date=August 30, 2004 | magazine=Time| first=Sarah M. | last=Broom | name-list-style = vanc }}</ref> Motivated by the lack of instruction in medical schools on the subject of death and dying, Kübler-Ross examined death and those faced with it at the [[University of Chicago]]'s [[Pritzker School of Medicine|medical school]]. Kübler-Ross's project evolved into a series of seminars which, along with patient interviews and previous research, became the foundation for her book.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.uky.edu/~cperring/kr.htm |title=PHI350: The Stages in the Dying Process |first=Christian |last=Perring | name-list-style = vanc |access-date=November 27, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161115151944/http://www.uky.edu/~cperring/kr.htm |archive-date=2016-11-15 |url-status=dead}}</ref> Although Kübler-Ross is commonly credited with creating stage models, earlier bereavement theorists and clinicians such as [[Erich Lindemann]], [[Colin Murray Parkes|Collin Murray Parkes]], and [[John Bowlby]] used similar models of stages or phases as early as the 1940s.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Bereavement groups and the role of social support: integrating theory, research, and practice |last=Hoy |first=William G. | name-list-style = vanc |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge/Taylor and Francis |isbn=978-1317416357 |location=New York |oclc=942843686}}</ref> In the foreword to the first 1970 English edition of ''On Death and Dying'', Colin Murray Parkes wrote, "This book describes how some American individuals have coped with death."<ref>{{Cite book |last=Parkes |first=C Murray |title=(Forward for) On Death & Dying |date=1970 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=0415040159 |edition=First |location=London |pages=viii |language=English}}</ref>

In her book, Kübler-Ross states that the medical advancements of the time were the mark of change for the way people perceive and experience death.<ref name=":0" /> Due to this, pediatricians have been seeing fewer life-threatening ailments for their patients compared to one hundred years ago.<ref name=":0" />

In her 1974 book ''Questions and Answers on Death and Dying'', Kübler-Ross had by then observed that the stages are not experienced in a strictly linear progression. She noted, "Most of my patients have exhibited two or three stages simultaneously, and these do not always occur in the same order."<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kübler-Ross |first=Elisabeth |title=Questions and Answers On Death and Dying |date=1974 |publisher=Simon and Schuster |isbn=0025671200 |edition=First |location=New York, NY |publication-date=1974 |pages=25, 26 |language=English}}</ref> She later regretted writing them in a way that was misunderstood.<ref name=GriefGrieving /> "Kübler-Ross originally saw these stages as reflecting how people cope with illness and dying," observed grief researcher Kenneth J. Doka, "not as reflections of how people grieve."<ref>{{cite book |last=Doka |first=Kenneth J. |name-list-style=vanc |title=Grief Is a Journey: Finding Your Path Through Loss |publisher=Simon and Schuster |date=2016 |page=6 }}</ref> In the 1980s, the Five Stages of Grief evolved into the Kübler-Ross Change Curve, which is now widely utilized by companies to navigate and manage organizational change and loss.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2015-06-24 |title=Understanding the Kubler-Ross Change Curve |url=https://www.cleverism.com/understanding-kubler-ross-change-curve/ |access-date=2024-08-19 |website=Cleverism |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Tahir |first=Umar |date=2019-12-10 |title=What is Kubler Ross Change Curve? {{!}} Stages & Advantages |url=https://changemanagementinsight.com/kubler-ross-change-curve-model/ |access-date=2024-08-19 |website=CMI |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-05-12 |title=Top 10 change management models: A comparison guide |url=https://www.zendesk.com/blog/change-management-models/ |access-date=2024-08-19 |website=Zendesk |language=en-US}}</ref> 

As of 2019, ''On Death and Dying'' has been translated into forty-one languages, with the 50th anniversary edition published by [[Simon & Schuster]]. In December 2019, ''The American Journal of Bioethics'' published a special issue (Volume 19, Number 12) dedicated to commemorating the 50th anniversary of its publication.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kuczewski |first=Mark G. |date=2019-12-02 |title=Everything I Really Needed to Know to Be a Clinical Ethicist, I Learned From Elisabeth Kübler-Ross* |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15265161.2019.1674410 |journal=The American Journal of Bioethics |language=en |volume=19 |issue=12 |pages=13–18 |doi=10.1080/15265161.2019.1674410 |pmid=31746704 |issn=1526-5161|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Saracino |first1=Rebecca M. |last2=Rosenfeld |first2=Barry |last3=Breitbart |first3=William |last4=Chochinov |first4=Harvey Max |date=2019-12-02 |title=Psychotherapy at the End of Life |journal=The American Journal of Bioethics |language=en |volume=19 |issue=12 |pages=19–28 |doi=10.1080/15265161.2019.1674552 |issn=1526-5161 |pmc=6986450 |pmid=31746703}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |date=December 1, 2019 |title=The American Journal of Bioethics: Special Issue: 50th Anniversary of "ON Death & Dying" by Elisabeth Kübler-Ross |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/toc/uajb20/19/12 |journal=The American Journal of Bioethics |volume=19 |issue=12 |pages=1–77}}</ref>

== Stages of grief ==
[[File:Kübler Ross grieving curve (edited).svg|thumb|A diagram developed by Bertrand Grondin from a presentation of Kübler-Ross' ideas produced by [[France Telecom]]]]
[[File:Kübler Ross's stages of grief.svg|thumb|Diagram showing two possible outcomes of grief or a life-changing event]]

Kübler-Ross originally developed stages to describe the process patients with terminal illness go through as they come to terms with their own deaths; it was later applied to grieving friends and family as well, who seemed to undergo a similar process.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Feldman |first1=David B | name-list-style = vanc |title=Why the Five Stages of Grief Are Wrong |url=https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/supersurvivors/201707/why-the-five-stages-grief-are-wrong |website=Psychology Today |access-date=15 May 2018 |date=7 July 2017 |archive-url=https://megalodon.jp/2026-0505-1007-55/https://www.psychologytoday.com:443/us/blog/supersurvivors/201707/why-the-five-stages-grief-are-wrong |archive-date=2026-05-04 |url-status=live}}</ref> The stages, popularly known by the acronym DABDA, include:<ref name="Santrock">{{cite book |title=A Topical Approach to Life-Span Development | vauthors = Santrock JW |year= 2007 |publisher= [[McGraw-Hill]] |location= New York |isbn= 978-0-07-338264-7 }}{{page needed|date=May 2014}}</ref>
# [[Denial]] – The first reaction is denial. In this stage, individuals believe the precipitating event is somehow mistaken, and cling to a false, preferable reality. Some may also isolate themselves, avoiding others who may have accepted what is happening.<ref name=":0" /> This stage is usually a temporary defense, so long as the person has adequate time to move amongst the stages as they contemplate death.<ref name=":0" /> In her book, Kübler-Ross states that technological advancements have caused people to become fearful of violent, painful deaths; therefore, in order to protect the psychological mind, they deny the reality of their own inevitable death.<ref name=":0" />
# [[Anger]] – When the individual recognizes that denial cannot continue, they become [[Frustration|frustrated]], especially at proximate individuals. Certain psychological responses of a person undergoing this phase would be: "Why me? It's not fair!"; "How can this happen to me?"; "Who is to blame?"; "Why would this happen?". Some may lash out at loved ones, medical staff, and other family.<ref name=":0" /> In Kübler-Ross's other book, ''Questions and Answers on Death and Dying'', she emphasizes the need for people to do their best to let those who are in this stage feel their feelings and try not to take the anger personally.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Kübler-Ross |first=Elisabeth |title=Questions and Answers on Death and Dying |publisher=Macmillan |year=1974 |isbn=0025671200}}</ref>
# [[Bargaining]] – The third stage involves the hope that the individual can avoid a cause of grief. Usually, the negotiation for an extended life is made in exchange for a reformed lifestyle. People facing less serious trauma can bargain or seek compromise. Examples include the terminally ill person who "negotiates with God" to attend a daughter's wedding, an attempt to bargain for more time to live in exchange for a reformed lifestyle or a phrase such as "If I could trade their life for mine".
# [[Depression (mood)|Depression]] – "I'm so sad, why bother with anything?"; "I'm going to die soon, so what's the point?"; "I miss my loved one; why go on?" During the fourth stage, the individual despairs at the recognition of their mortality. In this state, the individual may become silent, refuse visitors and spend much of the time mournful and sullen.
# [[Acceptance]] – "It's going to be okay."; "I can't fight it; I may as well prepare for it." In this last stage, individuals embrace mortality or inevitable future, or that of a loved one, or other tragic event. People dying may precede the survivors in this state, which typically comes with a calm, retrospective view for the individual, and a stable condition of emotions.
Kübler-Ross identified additional stages of emotional response beyond the five widely recognized stages of grief, illustrated in a full-page graphic on page 251 of the 50th anniversary edition of ''On Death and Dying''. Alongside the well-known stages of denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance, Kübler-Ross detailed other "stages" such as shock, partial denial, preparatory grief (also known as [[anticipatory grief]]), hope, and decathexis, which refers to the process of withdrawing emotional investment from external objects or relationships.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kübler-Ross |first=Elisabeth |title=On Death & Dying What the Dying Have to Teach Doctors, Nurses, Clergy & Their Own Families |date=2019 |publisher=Simon and Schuster |isbn=9781476775548 |pages=251}}</ref> She also acknowledged other emotional responses including guilt, anxiety, and numbness.

In ''Questions and Answers on Death and Dying'', Kübler-Ross answered questions after the publication of her first book, ''On Death and Dying''. She emphasized that no patient should be directly told that they are dying and that practitioners should try to wait until the patient asks about death to discuss it.<ref name=":1" /> In her book, she also proposes that practitioners listen to the patient first and foremost, and that the patient's right to [[self-determination]] should still be practiced.<ref name=":1" />

In a posthumously published book co-authored with [[David Kessler (writer)|David Kessler]], Kübler-Ross expanded the model to address a wide range of personal losses, recognizing that it might be more about change than solely about grief. This broader framework, more commonly known as the Kübler-Ross Change Curve,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-06-18 |title=The Kubler Ross Change Curve: Understanding The 5 Stages Of Change |url=https://themindsjournal.com/kubler-ross-change-curve/ |access-date=2024-08-19 |website=themindsjournal.com |language=en-US}}</ref> encompasses various forms of loss, including the death of a loved one, job or income loss, major rejection, relationship breakups or divorce, drug addiction, the onset of illness or [[infertility]], and even minor setbacks like losing insurance coverage.<ref name="GriefGrieving">{{Cite book |title=On grief & grieving : finding the meaning of grief through the five stages of loss |last1=Kübler-Ross |first1=Elisabeth |date=2014 |publisher=Scribner |last2=Kessler |first2=David | name-list-style = vanc |isbn=978-1476775555 |location=New York |oclc=863077888}}</ref> Kessler has also proposed "Meaning" as a sixth stage of grief.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H920DwAAQBAJ |title = Finding Meaning: The Sixth Stage of Grief|isbn = 978-1501192739|last1 = Kessler|first1 = David | name-list-style = vanc |date = 5 November 2019| publisher=Simon and Schuster }}</ref> Other authors have also explored and expanded upon stage theories, such as Claire Bidwell Smith in her book ''Anxiety: The Missing Stage of Grief'', which addresses additional aspects of emotional response and adjustment beyond Kübler-Ross's original framework.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Anxiety: The Missing Stage of Grief |url=https://clairebidwellsmith.com/anxiety-missing-stage-grief/ |access-date=2024-08-19 |website=Claire Bidwell Smith |language=en-US}}</ref>

In 2020, during the [[COVID-19 pandemic]], Kessler applied the five stages to responses to the virus, saying: "It's not a map but it provides some scaffolding for this unknown world."

{{blockquote|"There's denial, which we saw a lot of early on: ''This virus won't affect us.'' There's anger: ''You're making me stay home and taking away my activities.'' There's bargaining: ''Okay, if I social distance for two weeks everything will be better, right?'' There's sadness: ''I don't know when this will end.'' And finally there's acceptance. ''This is happening; I have to figure out how to proceed.''<br><br>Acceptance, as you might imagine, is where the power lies. We find control in acceptance. ''I can wash my hands. I can keep a safe distance. I can learn how to work virtually''."<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Berinato|first=Scott|title=That Discomfort You're Feeling Is Grief|url=https://hbr.org/2020/03/that-discomfort-youre-feeling-is-grief|access-date=3 July 2020|journal=Harvard Business Review|date=23 March 2020}}</ref>}}

==Criticism==
Though widely used, the theory is empirically unsupported, potentially harmful, and of limited practical value; alternative models more accurately reflect grieving processes.<ref name="Stroebe2017"/> 

Robert J. Kastenbaum (1932–2013) who was a recognized expert in [[gerontology]], aging, and death, raised the following points:<ref>{{cite book |last=Kastenbaum |first=Robert |name-list-style=vanc |title=Death, Society, and Human Experience |edition=6th |location=Boston |publisher=Allyn & Bacon |date=1998 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Corr | first1 = Charles A. | first2 = Kenneth J. | last2 = Doka | first3 = Robert | last3 = Kastenbaum | name-list-style = vanc | date = 1999 | title = Dying and Its Interpreters: A Review of Selected Literature and Some Comments on the State of the Field. | journal = Omega: Journal of Death and Dying | volume = 39 | issue = 4 | pages = 239–259 | doi = 10.2190/3KGF-52BV-QTNT-UBMX | s2cid = 145434532 }}</ref>
* The existence of these stages as such has not been demonstrated.
* No evidence has been presented that people actually do move from Stage 1 through Stage 5.
* The limitations of the method have not been acknowledged.
* The line is blurred between description and prescription.
* The resources, pressures, and characteristics of the immediate environment, which can make a tremendous difference, are not taken into account.

A widely cited 2003 study of bereaved individuals conducted by Maciejewski and colleagues at [[Yale University]] obtained some findings consistent with a five-stage hypothesis but others inconsistent with it. Several letters were also published in the same journal criticizing this research and arguing against the stage idea.<ref name="Jama">{{cite journal | vauthors = Maciejewski PK, Zhang B, Block SD, Prigerson HG | title = An empirical examination of the stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 7 | pages = 716–723 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 17312291 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.7.716 | doi-access = free }}</ref> It was pointed out, for example, that instead of "acceptance" being the final stage of grieving, the data actually showed it was the most frequently endorsed item at the first and every other time point measured;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bonanno GA, Boerner K | title = The stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 24 | pages = 2693; author reply 2693–4 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17595267 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.24.2693-a }}</ref> that cultural and geographical bias within the sample population was not controlled for;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Weiner JS | title = The stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 24 | pages = 2692–93; author reply 2693–4 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17595265 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.24.2692-b }}</ref> and that out of the total number of participants originally recruited for the study, nearly 40% were excluded from the analysis who did not fit the stage model.<ref name="Silver"/> In subsequent work, Prigerson & Maciejewski focused on acceptance (emotional and cognitive) and backed away from stages, writing that their earlier results "might more accurately be described as 'states' of grief."<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Prigerson HG, Maciejewski PK | title = Grief and acceptance as opposite sides of the same coin: setting a research agenda to study peaceful acceptance of loss | journal = The British Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 193 | issue = 6 | pages = 435–437 | date = December 2008 | pmid = 19043142 | doi = 10.1192/bjp.bp.108.053157 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

[[George Bonanno]], Professor of [[Clinical psychology|Clinical Psychology]] at [[Columbia University]], in his book ''The Other Side of Sadness: What the New Science of Bereavement Tells Us About Life After a Loss'',<ref name="Bonanno2009">{{cite book|last=Bonanno|first=George | name-list-style = vanc |author-link=George Bonanno |title=The Other Side of Sadness: What the New Science of Bereavement Tells Us about Life After Loss|url=https://archive.org/details/othersideofsadne00bona|url-access=registration|year=2009|publisher=Basic Books|isbn=978-0-465-01360-9}}</ref> summarizes [[peer-review]]ed research based on thousands of subjects over two decades and concludes that a natural [[psychological resilience]] is a principal component of grief<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bonanno GA | title = Loss, trauma, and human resilience: have we underestimated the human capacity to thrive after extremely aversive events? | journal = The American Psychologist | volume = 59 | issue = 1 | pages = 20–28 | date = January 2004 | pmid = 14736317 | doi = 10.1037/0003-066X.59.1.20 | url = http://www.public.asu.edu/~iacmao/PGS191/Resilience%20Reading%20%231A.pdf }}</ref> and that there are no stages of grief to pass. Bonanno's work has also demonstrated that absence of grief or trauma symptoms is a healthy outcome.<ref name="scientificamerican1">{{cite journal | vauthors = Stix G | title = The neuroscience of true grit | journal = Scientific American | volume = 304 | issue = 3 | pages = 28–33 | date = March 2011 | pmid = 21438486 | doi = 10.1038/scientificamerican0311-28 | bibcode = 2011SciAm.304c..28S }}</ref><ref name="timemag"/>

Among social scientists, another criticism is a lack of theoretical underpinning.<ref name="Stroebe2017"/><ref name="Corr2018">{{Cite journal| vauthors = Corr CA |date=23 October 2018|title=The 'five stages' in coping with dying and bereavement: strengths, weaknesses and some alternatives|journal=Mortality|volume=24|issue=4|pages=405–417|doi=10.1080/13576275.2018.1527826|s2cid=149545381}}</ref> Because the stages arose from anecdotes and not underlying theoretical principles it contains conceptual confusion. For example, some people criticize that some stages represent emotions while others represent cognitive processes, as if the experience felt must discern one from another. Also, there is no rationale for arbitrary dividing lines between states. On the other hand, there are other theoretically based, scientific perspectives that better represent the course of grief and bereavement such as: [[George Bonanno#Trajectories of grief and trauma reactions|trajectories approach]], [[Psychological stress|cognitive stress theory]], [[Meaning-making#In bereavement|meaning-making approach]], psychosocial transition model, [[Grief#Grieving process|two-track model]], [[Dual process model of coping|dual process model]], and the task model.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Stroebe MS, Schut H |chapter=Models of coping with bereavement: A review | veditors = Stroebe MS, Hansson RO, Stroebe W, Schut H |title=Handbook of bereavement research: Consequences, coping, and care |date=2001 |publisher=American Psychological Association Press |location=Washington, DC |pages=375–403 |isbn=1-55798-736-X |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2001-18149-000}}</ref>

Misapplication can be harmful if it leads bereaved persons to feel that they are not coping appropriately or it can result in ineffective support by members of their [[social network]] and/or health care professionals.<ref name="Stroebe2017"/><ref name="Silver">{{cite journal | vauthors = Silver RC, Wortman CB | title = The stage theory of grief | journal = JAMA | volume = 297 | issue = 24 | pages = 2692; author reply 2693–4 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17595266 | doi = 10.1001/jama.297.24.2692-a }}</ref> The stages were originally meant to be descriptive but over time became prescriptive. Some caregivers dealt with clients who were distressed that they did not experience the stages in "the right order" or failed to experience one or more of the stages of grief.

Criticism and lack of support in peer-reviewed research or objective clinical observation by some practitioners in the field has led to the labels of ''myth'' and ''fallacy'' in the notion that there are stages of grief.<ref name="timemag">{{cite news|url=http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,2042372-2,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110131162817/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,2042372-2,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=January 31, 2011|title=New Ways to Think About Grief|first=Ruth Davis|last=Konigsberg | name-list-style = vanc |date=January 29, 2011|access-date=27 November 2016|via=www.time.com}}</ref><ref name="Corr2018"/><ref name="Shermer">{{cite web |last1=Shermer |first1=Michael | name-list-style = vanc |title=Five Fallacies of Grief: Debunking Psychological Stages |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/five-fallacies-of-grief/ |website=www.scientificamerican.com |publisher=[[Scientific American]] |date=1 November 2008 |access-date=29 December 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200511014903/https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/five-fallacies-of-grief/ |archive-date=11 May 2020 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wortman CB, Silver RC | title = The myths of coping with loss | journal = Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology | volume = 57 | issue = 3 | pages = 349–357 | date = June 1989 | pmid = 2661609 | doi = 10.1037/0022-006x.57.3.349 }}</ref> Nevertheless, the model's use has persisted in popular news and entertainment media, and some professionals have stated their confidence in the veracity of the model.

Kübler-Ross acknowledged the variability and complexity of individual experiences, using the so-called "stages" of emotional responses as a framework to describe common patterns. She explicitly described these stages as a heuristic device, noting that they are categories artificially isolated for clarity, with the understanding that emotional responses are fluid and overlapping.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kellehear |first=Professor Allan |title=On Death & Dying: 40th Anniversary Edition |publisher=Routledge |year=2009 |isbn=9780415463997 |edition=40th Anniversary |location=London |pages=vii-xvi}}</ref> In her book, Kübler-Ross repeatedly warned that these "stages" can overlap, occur simultaneously, or be missed altogether, and she even placed the term "stages" in inverted commas in the book's diagrammatic representation to emphasize their tentative nature.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kellehear |first=Professor Allan |title=On Death & Dying: What the Dying Have too Teach Doctors, Nurses, Clergy and Their Own Families |publisher=Routledge |year=2009 |isbn=9780415463997 |edition=40th Anniversary |location=London}}</ref> The principal aim of ''On Death and Dying'' was to fundamentally reshape attitudes toward the experiences of dying patients by advocating for a more humane and patient-centered approach in medical practice and beyond, rather than merely defining the experience of dying in "stages".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Block |first=Dr. Ira |title=On Death & Dying - 50th Anniversary |date=2019 |publisher=Simon & Schuster |isbn=9781476775548 |edition=50th Anniversary |location=New York |publication-date=2019 |pages=xi-xv}}</ref>

* {{cite book | vauthors = Scire P | date = 2007 | title = Applying Grief Stages to Organizational Change }}
* {{cite book | first = Johannes Hendrikus | last = Van der Poel | name-list-style = vanc | title = An Evaluation of the Relevance of the Kübler-Ross Model to the Post-injury Responses of Competitive Athletes | publisher = [[University of the Free State]] | date = 2000 }}
{{refend}}
